text: Adhesions are visible between the anterior abdominal wall and underlying tissues, likely due to the patient's previous cesarean section.
The surgeon performs adhesiolysis to remove the omentum, carefully separating it from the anterior abdominal wall.
The surgeon observes the onset of gangrene in the tissue, with visible fluid accumulation in the affected area.
The surgeon uses two atraumatic graspers to perform detorsion of the ovary, carefully manipulating the ovary to restore its normal position.

The surgeon corrects the torsion and punctures a hemorrhagic cyst using a harmonic device. The inner cystic layer is visible and can be separated, followed by thorough lavage to remove the hemorrhagic content.
Two Maryland forceps are used to hold the inner cystic layer of the ovarian cyst, while the outer layer is being carefully separated.
The cystic content is placed in the anterior cul-de-sac. It appears friable and can be removed using a stone holding forceps or an endo bag.
The surgeon removes the cyst following impaired blood supply due to torsion, and performs irrigation and suction to clear the surgical field.
The surgeon observes adhesions between the omentum and the anterior abdominal wall, which may have contributed to the torsion.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to carefully separate adhesions surrounding the cyst. Once the adhesions are cleared, the cyst is prepared for removal, followed by thorough lavage to cleanse the area.
The surgeon performs plication of the elongated ovarian ligament using 2-0 Prolene sutures to prevent future torsion.

The surgeon uses a needle holder to take several consecutive bites on the ovarian ligament, resembling a purse string suture technique. A grasper provides lateral traction on the ovary during this three-port laparoscopic surgery.
The surgeon tightens an extracorporeal square knot to achieve plication of the ovarian ligament, using multiple knots with proline suture to ensure stability and prevent slipping.
The surgeon secures the suture with three to four intracorporeal knots to prevent slipping, followed by cutting the excess suture material.
The surgeon performs a thorough lavage of the surgical area, ensuring all debris and fluids are cleared before concluding the procedure.
